briefing.opening: Hello everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for May 29, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Global Climate Policy Retreat and Increased Uncertainty
sections.content: The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) is moving to abolish regulations requiring companies to report greenhouse gas emissions and climate risks, thereby increasing uncertainty in international climate policy [20, 21, 22, 33, 34]. This move, which would revoke significant climate-related regulations from the Biden administration era, could cause substantial setbacks in climate change response efforts. Furthermore, Sherrill is planning delays for groundbreaking climate change regulations [23], and EM-DAT, a global disaster database relied upon by thousands of climate scientists, is even facing closure [42], raising concerns about the weakening of international access to climate information and the basis for policy decisions. Americans also harbor doubts about whether nations are doing enough to curb climate change [24], shaking overall confidence in climate governance.

sections.title: ⚠️ Deepening Climate Crisis and Complex Impacts
sections.content: The climate crisis is having complex impacts globally, accompanied by serious warnings that it could transform the international order into a 'new imperialism' [1, 3]. In Europe, May heatwaves have escalated into a warning of the climate crisis [6, 37, 40], leading to a severe situation where areas uninsurable due to wildfires and floods are expanding [26]. Climate change is threatening global plant species by shrinking their habitats [28] and is suspected of contributing to a surge in hantavirus cases in Argentina [30, 31]. News that the AI boom is leading to increased investment in fossil fuel power generation in the U.S. [41] illustrates the paradox of energy transition, and at COP31, arguments have been made that nations must be persuaded to develop fossil fuel transition plans [29]. Furthermore, reports that deadly heat during the Hajj is being exacerbated by climate change [39] add to concerns about human casualties.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Climate Change Response Efforts and Challenges
sections.content: Domestically, various efforts to address climate change have been observed, alongside some challenges. Jeonbuk Jinan County is supplying joint pest control agents for sudden outbreaks [4], and climate insurance support for heatwave damages is being provided [5], making climate change adaptation efforts visible. Prime Minister Kim mentioned achievements in expanding solar power and electric vehicle adoption [8], and the Ministry of Foreign Affairs is continuing policy efforts, such as introducing renewable energy industrial sites to resident diplomatic corps [9, 12]. Korea Western Power received the Industrial Promotion Grand Prize for its contributions to renewable energy expansion [10, 11, 13, 15, 19], and Gwangju University is fostering talent through on-site training in new and renewable energy [18]. However, issues such as confusion over investment targets for 'climate change ETFs' [2] and the halving of carbon neutrality points when purchasing eco-friendly agricultural products [7] suggest the need for enhanced policy consistency and transparency.

sections.title: 🌱 International Trends in Climate Adaptation and Renewable Energy Expansion
sections.content: In the international community, innovative attempts to adapt to climate change and expand renewable energy are ongoing. The wine industry is employing various innovative strategies to adapt to climate change [27, 32], serving as an important example for strengthening climate resilience in the agricultural sector. Furthermore, efforts to raise awareness through community and youth engagement are active, such as media students at the University of Zimbabwe leading wetland dialogues to enhance climate change communication [38]. In Nigeria, the deployment of solar mini-grids is gaining attention [40], and countries are actively moving to adopt and expand renewable energy technologies, with China focusing on large-scale urban energy solutions including district heating, solar power, electric vehicles, and hydrogen [14]. These efforts serve as positive signals in the global response to the climate crisis.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlighted the deepening climate crisis amidst concerns of global policy setbacks, alongside various ongoing adaptation and transition efforts both domestically and internationally. International solidarity and consistent policy implementation are more crucial than ever to address the climate crisis. This concludes the climate briefing for May 29, 2026.
